[IMAGE:0]It's midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Walter B. Gibson, one of the few professional stage magicians who was also a genuine occultist -- his 1966 book The Complete Illustrated Book of the Psychic Sciences, written with his wife Litzka, is a good summary of the state of popular occultism on the eve of the 1970s occult revival -- and also, of course, the pulp writer responsible for that iconic figure, The Shadow. ("Who knows what evil lurks in the hearts of men? The Shadow knows...")

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. 

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Depends on what kind of training and experience in magic you have. replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
First, please do take the time to make and use a set of Druid wands for your geomancy readings. Yes, you can also do dice, and it's good to learn various ways to cast geomantic charts, but when following a course of initiatory training, it's wise to follow the instructions to the letter.

Second, if you're using the shield chart, the right witness is you, the left witness is your day, and the judge is how the day affects you. The time factor in a daily divination is less important, since you've already set the specific time interval for which you're casting the chart.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've noticed the same thing, and I think what we're seeing is world-class cognitive dissonance. The world is supposed to behave in X way, according to the accepted ideologies, but it's not -- and the more obviously it's not, the more loudly believers insist that X must be true and any evidence that it isn't true must be fake news, etc. Back of the whole thing, I suspect, is the cascading failure of the myth of progress, the primary religious belief of our time -- that's generating vast amounts of cognitive dissonance as people try to square their faith in perpetual progress with the rising tide of evidence that modern industrial society is in accelerating decline. Off=brand soda pop is probably a good response; when you've got someone deep in cognitive dissonance, "back away from the crazy person" is about the only advice that works.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have a few questions:

First, last week, you mentioned "lords of karma" in a ritual you provided. What exactly are they?

Second, what exactly is karma? I know it's the consequences of past actions, but what does that entail?

Third, how could I go about breaking an addiction? Sheer willpower doesn't seem to be enough, so should I strengthen it, or is there something else that can be done?

Finally, I don't dream, or at least I never remember my dreams. Do you know any ways to change that?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There's a magical axiom that runs "every energy is also an entity, and every entity is also an energy." That is, you can experience the phenomena of the spiritual world as impersonal forces or as personalized beings, and both of these are valid ways of experiencing them. The Lords of Karma are the spiritual structures of karma experienced as personalized beings. What exactly they are is hard to say, but they seem to be of the order of things that old-fashioned occultists call "archangelic."

2) Karma works through character. "Sow a thought and reap a word, sow a word and reap a deed, sow a deed and reap a habit, sow a habit and reap a character, sow a character and reap a destiny." Every thought, word, and deed you engage in shapes who you are, and who you are attracts certain experiences to you and repels certain other experiences. There's no one sitting in judgment -- you are, through the way your actions shape who you are, your own judge, jury, and executioner.

3) You need to understand the role that the addiction plays in your emotional life. Journaling is a good way to do this. Get a notebook or blank book and a pen, and start engaging in written dialogues with your addiction. Ask it to remind you why you have it, what it does for you, and so on. Let it remind you of memories you've forgotten and issues you have tried not to deal with. The best way to break an addiction is to outgrow it, and you outgrow it by figuring out what it does for you.

4) Sure. Repeat the affirmation "I remember my dreams" nine times silently before going to bed each night. Keep a journal and pen by your bedside, and when you begin to recall the first faint scraps of your dreams, write down a description of everything you can remember. Keep doing that and in time you'll be remembering detailed dreams.

replies.content:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) We don't know what exactly any class of spiritual being is; it's fair to say that we don't know exactly what we are! You'll find scraps of lore about archangels in most old-fashioned occult texts.

2) No, not at all. What you're asking for, if you do the sort of working I outlined last week, is a set of experiences traumatic enough to force a rapid change in character.

replies.content: It is incredibly risky. Unless you know what your karma amounts to -- and that takes quite a bit of self-knowledge and insight -- you have no way of knowing what you're setting yourself up for; thus my comment to the person who asked, that he might be asking to be thrown in prison for a crime he didn't commit and left to rot there until he died, or something equally charming.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Wouldn't anyone with that kind of self knowledge usually be able to change it without the ritual?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes. You can deal with karma in any of three ways. You can work it out on the material plane, by slamming into one brick wall of misery after another until finally it hurts too much not to change; you can work it out on the planes of personality -- what we call the aetherial plane in the Druidical Order of the GD -- by tackling your negative impulses within yourself rather than letting them flow out into the world around you; or you can work it out on the spiritual planes by an act of realization and repentance -- that latter word, btw, doesn't mean feeling bad about what you've done, it means understanding the causes of the act in such a way that the act is no longer an option for you. (Actual repentance is a form of enlightenment.)

So, yeah, if you've achieved the kind of self-knowledge and insight that will show you your karma, you're not going to need to work it out on the material plane.

comments.title: The sound
comments.content:
JMG, I was wondering if you could help me categorize, if possible, a clairaudient experience I've had several in my life, the first having occurred when I was an adolescent, probably around 10 or so - I woke up in middle of night hearing what sounded like a rhythmic grinding of gears, a sort of ghastly anti-music. The sound persisted though I was fully awake, and I recall imaging basement machinery coming up the stairs toward my bedroom. I dearly wanted to fall back to sleep and escape it, but every time I began to drift off, the sound would grow louder. I then found that by remaining as alert and attentive as possible, I could get the sound to fade away to nothing. (this tactic helped me immensely in later years when I was confronted by fear and anxiety).

Approx 40 later, I awoke one morning from a dream hearing something of the same godawful sound, though this time it faded away in moments. I think it's impossible to accurately describe the sheer awfulness of this sound in words, save that it seems "anti-life", the antithesis of, say, the music of the spheres. I think it would be impossible to replicate with any kind of material tools or instruments.

Now, I've had many psychic experiences in my day, and I'd say this one pretty much covers the low end of the spectrum. But what exactly was I hearing? I realize the experience could be unique to me, but I have the disturbing impression that this sound may be there all the time transmitting away like some kind of lower-astral beacon and that everyone might be hearing it and be impacted by it on some level of consciousness - perhaps only occasionally does it break through to waking consciousness. I've tried to research it and thus far, I've come up with zip. Do you have any idea on just what might be generating this seemingly insanity-inducing sound? I was wondering if any of your readers might have experienced something similar .... thank you. - Will M

replies.title: Re: Geomancy to detect magical attack
replies.content:
Okay, first, it's not usually a good idea to interpret one reading using both the shield chart and the house chart. I'm going to take the house chart as the basis for my interpretation here, because it's better suited to yes/no questions.

In the house chart, the significator of the querent is Fortuna Minor and the significator of the quesited is Tristitia, the figure in the eighth house, which is the house of magic. (Sixth is the house of health, not of sorcery.) Tristitia looks dire, right? But any time you ask a yes/no question of a house chart, the answer is shown by whether there's a mode of perfection linking the figure of the querent and that of the quesited. There is none. Fortuna Minor passes to the fourth to trine the eighth, but an aspect won't usually perfect a chart all by itself -- especially when you're asking about something unwelcome and it's a favorable aspect. So the answer is no, nobody's working evil against you.

(If it were a sixth house question, the answer would still be no, because no mode of perfection links Fortuna Minor with Cauda Draconis, either.)

Let's put the "no" in context by looking at the Judge and Witnesses, which are read in both kinds of charts. What that says is that something has happened to you (Acquisitio) which is also happening to lots of other people (Populus), but you mistakenly took it personally (Acquisitio again). Fortunately, as you've noted, the cardines show that it'll all work out for you, though there will be some delays involved (very common when Fortuna Major shows up in the 4th -- "success after delay" is a good summary of that figure's meaning.

comments.title: Some vid / podcast suggestions
comments.content:
Greetings, and congratulations on your part in helping the Earth complete another formalized circuit around the sun this week!

This isn't a question but I thought I'd call your attention to a couple of videos and podcasts and offer you the opportunity to comment, if desired.

Alan Moore, famous fringe writer whom most people probably know here, defines Magic as "Any purposeful engagement with the phenomena and possibilities of consciousness", and connects it in a fundamental, primordial way to all of art and culture, by thinking about the figure of the shaman. His definition is broader than the definition you usually promote, but I think it's kinda cool.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RtbBmIfLYbk

Alan Moore comes to this definition by noting that even dreams and feelings, even the realization of the passage of time and the cyclical nature of the seasons -- to a hominid with emerging, developing consciousness, must have seemed like they came from an outside supernatural source. He cites "The Origin of Consciousness in the Breakdown of the Bicameral Mind," a book I'd bet dollars to donuts you're familiar with, but just in case you or anybody else is not familiar with the book -- I haven't read the whole thing, unfortunately, but I've read a lot of clips and summaries... I'll make a pathetic attempt to summarize it by proposing that its central theory states that the instinctive and procedural portions of the hominid brain separated from each other as they specialized, and the instinctive portions became in essence religious (answering the question of the _why_ of the world, with inscrutable dictates: "Take Shelter!" "Hunt for Food!"), whereas the procedural portion ("_how_ do I take shelter from this storm? That cave? _How_ do I hunt that animal? With a rock or my bare hands") became the portion of human experience that we now refer to as intelligence and consciousness.

The book and its theory, I hear, has fallen out of favor with both psychologists and those who study intelligence (such as A.I. researchers), mainly I suppose because it relies on left-brain/right-brain theory which most people consider outdated.

I also ran across another podcast by two people, Phil Ford and J. F. Martel, who sometimes drop hints that they study magical theory while also discussing pop culture and zeitgeist (they even have a Lovecraft episode)... They have dropped the Golden Dawn definition of Magic at least a couple times (I believe this is one of them, where my friend Michael Garfield was the guest: https://www.weirdstudies.com/26 ), and one of their recent podcasts seems to run through your entire Pepe/Kek analysis without attributing you ( https://www.weirdstudies.com/36 ). Maybe you should check if they're readers of your site who "forgot" to give you credit??

I just noticed your recent appearance on the Emerge! podcast, and it reminded me that I was first introduced to you and your work by some podcasts (the Extra-Environmentalists and the C-Realm), but you haven't done a whole lot of podcast interviews recently. I'd love it if you'd make contact with Michael Garfield, host of the Future Fossils podcast ( https://www.patreon.com/michaelgarfield/posts ), because he's a friend of mine in person. I have suggested a few times that he reach out to you, and he seemed like he liked the idea, but he's been busy moving across the country so he probably hasn't done it yet. I'd love to hear the guys from Weird Studies take a look at your opus sometime, too! Preferably in the form of an interview.

replies.title: Re: Elemental Cross
replies.content:
A note on the Christian sign of the cross: Christians use both the left-to-right and the right-to-left sign of the cross. Touching the left shoulder first is typical of the Western church--Roman Catholics and their descendants. Touching the right shoulder first is typical of the Eastern church--both Byzantine Catholics and the Orthodox do it that way.

Christianity in the British isles had some Byzantine influence, and Anglicanism at times looked to the Eastern churches. Anglicans cross themselves both ways, depending on the person.

SamChevre

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
About nine months before he was born. ;-)

Aleister Crowley has long been my primary example of how not to be an occultist. He started out rich, handsome, talented, well-educated, and charismatic enough to get a steady string of lovers of both sexes tumbling into his bed; he ended up a burnt-out drug addict in a small-town flophouse with an estate worth 14 shillings and a name he had made into a laughing stock on three continents. If that's the fruit of a life spent in magic, um, you're doing it wrong.

He was also a very, very, very minor figure, a bit-part player, in the occult scene of his time. The main reason anyone remembers him at all today is that Grady McMurtry, who was a seriously talented occultist, picked up Crowley's fragmentary legacy and reshaped it into a successful system of initiation.

I encourage anyone who plans on studying magic seriously to read Crowley's autobiography and some of his magical writings, and then pick up The Magical Dilemma of Victor Neuburg and see what it the Not-So-Great Beast looked like from the other side of the mirror. Crowley's particular kind of egomaniacal dysfunction is far from uncommon in the magical scene these days, and getting a sense of how that dysfunction works is a good way to steer clear of it -- and of those who are mired in it.

comments.title: Metals
comments.content:
I am a metalworker. What would you say are the magical attributes of non-ferrous metals like nickel, aluminum, tin, copper, bronze, silver, or niobium? (I know I'm reaching there)

Also you mentioned something about identifying metals in some etheric way. How would one go about developing that ability? I've sometimes felt that I was near the being able to identify some metals by smell...

replies.title: Re: Metals
replies.content:
Different metals do smell differently, though it's a subtle thing and you have to train your sense of smell. It's a completely mundane thing, IMHO, not an occult one.

Also, the smell of various metals depends on how hot they are; if you are experienced enough to judge the temperature of a metal from its color, then its smell at that color gives you confirmation (or disconfirmation) as to which metal you are heating. I don't know enough to do this myself, but I know it can be done be experienced metal-workers.

There are other mundane senses that you can bring to bear on this problem as well. Try "ringing," that is, very gently striking, different metals against your teeth (your incisors are convenient and sensitive) and notice the rather striking differences in sensation. Try biting metals very gently, using your molars. The perceived results differ from one metal or alloy to the next.

These two tests--ringing and biting--probably depend on the molecular or crystaline structures of the metal being tested, as well as their hardness and density, but they seem to me to be reliable, and are said by real experts (experienced pawnbrokers, etc.) to give generally accurate results. I'm no expert, but I can make a few of the more simple identifications that way myself. (I was curious about this in my 'teens and twenties, and I started experimenting.)

Though the senses involved haven't got their own names in English, that doesn't mean they aren't genuine distinct physiological senses. If you want to limit your inventory of external senses to the traditional five, they're best subsumed under "touch" -- but it's a bad fit there.

By the way, ordinary human languages (English, German, Russian, Latin, Greek, etc.) make a rather bad job of it when they try to describe the more subtle aspects of the natural world. They are only "good enough for government work" (i.e. survival and reproduction), as the saying goes, but they're not all that good.

replies.title: Re: Metals
replies.content:
I once attended a talk by an occultist who was also a blacksmith, and would be happy to summarize:

The smith's view was that the magical properties of metals mostly had to do with their physical qualities: heat conductivity, electrical conductivity, magnetism, and density. Gold conducts very well - they make high-end electronics with it, and historically also high-end ritual tools even in places where gold was locally cheaper than silver. It's also dense, meaning it's good at retaining energetic patterns. Copper and silver are next best at conduction. Tin conducts poorly though it makes things more durable, so a bronze tool is inferior to a copper one but less likely to get damaged from heavy use. Lead doesn't conduct, it insulates - bad for energy-transmitting tools, good for containment - and it's dense - almost as good as gold for making talismans that hold a charge (see Greco-Roman 'curse tablets'!)

Iron is special due to magnetism. It's magnetized by electricity and demagnetized by heat, i.e. it grounds energy into the magnetosphere, i.e. it wipes away energy patterns the way a magnet can wipe a computer disk. However that's only 'cold iron' i.e. cold-hammered low-or-zero-carbon wrought iron, which is basically never made any more after quicker methods were developed in WWII to keep up with military demand. 'Iron' today is mostly carbon steel, and the higher-carbon it is the more it behaves like copper. If you want real 'cold iron' search the scrapyard for pre-1940 buggy parts, but handle with care!

Newer metals can be judged by the same standards. E.g. zinc (and thus brass) measures up similarly to tin, while aluminium ranks somewhere between high-end steel and low-end copper. He recommended getting a cheap conductivity meter to test your ritual tools! In theory the radioactive heavier-than-lead 'heavy metals' might make great talismans that held a charge long-term, but no-one wants to check ;). Titanium is odd, non-magnetic and electrically conductive (good) but not heat conductive (less good), and he expressed curiosity about how it would perform as e.g. a Wiccan athame.

Conductivity flows over the surface of metals, so a cheap metal plated with a better one is a decent compromise. Metals also pick up physical tracks from conducting energy long-term - it happens to e.g. wires - which is why metal tools imprint on their long-term users. The way to erase that is to re-temper the metal (reheat and cool it) or melt it down. If you want to physically clean something precious, Never-Dull from the auto care department is what museums use on fragile historical items.

replies.title: Re: Metals
replies.content:
"Metals also pick up physical tracks from conducting energy long-term - it happens to e.g. wires - which is why metal tools imprint on their long-term users."

I had a job doing repairs and maintenance to electronic and electromechanical equipment on the undercarriage of light rail cars. The steel shanks of my screwdrivers would get magnetized by the inductive currents of the electric motors (fans and pumps; also electrically powered switches of various kinds) operating next to where I was working. Having magnetized screwdrivers was handy for keeping itty bitty screws on the tip when trying to wire something I had to approach from an awkward angle.

replies.title: Re: Metals
replies.content:
Tin corresponds to Jupiter, copper to Venus, and silver to the Moon. Bronze is an alloy rather than a pure metal, so it has a mixed energy. Nickel, aluminum, and niobium were discovered after astrological correspondences dropped out of fashion, so they aren't in the old tables. I'd assign aluminum tentatively to Uranus, but I don't know enough about nickel and niobium to have a clue.

The best guide I know to developing the inner senses is W.E. Butler's collection How To Read The Aura, Practice Psychometry, Telepathy, and Clairvoyance. It doesn't give specific instructions for the kind of work you've mentioned, but psychometry and clairvoyance would be your launching points for developing that set of skills.

Attorneys I've interviewed are mixed in their assessment of our chances in court so we haven't made a final decision whether or not to pursue this legally. We hear through our family grapevine that, whatever the monetary profit from their actions, the thief and the two siblings aligned with her are not especially happy. Two are now divorced, the thief is stuck in a bad marriage because her husband was involved in the heist and they can't trust each other not to rat the other out. Nonetheless, I am still thinking of the promise I made to Mom.

I am not interested in revenge, nor am I particularly interested in winning scads of money - if that were even possible - but the rotten way Mom was treated really sticks in my craw and the possibility that they may actually get away with all this bothers me a lot. I thought of using a sweetening spell to take some of the steam out of their nastiness, but I don't know if that's the best approach. Any suggestions? Anyone?

replies.title: Re: Question
replies.content: It really does depend on what you decide to do, and what level of blowback you're willing to accept in your own life.

replies.title: Re: Question
replies.content:
IMHO, Anonymous, there's always hard blowback from anything one does to get Justice without doing roughly as much to bring Mercy into the outcome as well. It seems to bring out the worst in everyone.

Justice by itself has always seemed to me to be a double-edged sword with a poisoned handle: it wounds all parties to the dispute while also poisoning the person who dispenses it.

Of all the cards in the tarot pack, Justice is the one thst I have always felt to be the most vicious of them all, and am unhappiest to see turn up in a reading. Death, The Tower, The Hanged Man are all far more helpful to the querent in the long run (again, IMHO).

I suppose there's a philosophical lesson in this, at least as I see it: the Universe is not a place of Justice that we might live in. Any efforts to make it so go hugely against its very nature, and it fights back hard.

replies.title: Re: Question
replies.content:
I say this having been party to more than one complicated legal situation among family, where there were bad actors involved:

Let it go. It's only money/stuff.

You want justice. Justice WILL happen. You don't have to do anything to facilitate it, you may never get to know the details, and that's OK. Unearned wealth is its own punishment. People who act like the sibling in question... that doesn't end well, and the bad energy they generate is like a giant nasty fart that clings to them and everyone connected to them. You don't want that clinging to you, but that is what you're signing up for, if you insist on being the channel for that justice. Worse than that, really: you're giving them ownership of a part of yourself. Wouldn't you rather have your whole self available for things you love?

comments.title: Freemasonry

replies.title: Re: Freemasonry
replies.content: Soak it up like a sponge. Don't worry about teasing out the occult dimensions of it yet -- the experience of being entered, passed, and raised is the important thing at this stage. Regular attendance at lodge is very useful to soak yourself in the subtle energies of the Craft. Later on, keep your eyes open and see if you can figure out where the Masonic occultists in your jurisdiction hang out -- sometimes it's a lodge, sometimes it's one of the higher-degree bodies, but there's always someplace, though it may take you a while to find it or them a while to find you. Welcome to the Craft, Brother!
